heroOverview: Trichostrongyliasis is an intestinal nematode infection caused by Trichostrongylus species — slender, hair-like worms, 4-8 mm long, that inhabit the upper small intestine and duodenum of humans and herbivorous animals. Transmission occurs by accidentally ingesting infective larvae from contaminated raw vegetables or water; the worms are not transmitted person-to-person. An estimated 5.5 million people are infected globally, with the highest rates in rural communities in Iran, Iraq, Egypt, and other parts of the Middle East, as well as parts of Asia. Most infected people carry light worm burdens and are asymptomatic; heavier infections produce epigastric pain, nausea, diarrhoea, and a mild normocytic anaemia from intestinal blood loss. The condition is curable with a single dose of albendazole, mebendazole, or pyrantel pamoate.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 5.5 million people infected globally; highest endemicity in Iran, Iraq, and Egypt (WHO/CDC)
keyStats.demographics: Rural populations with agricultural exposure to animal faeces; all ages; children and farm workers most at risk
keyStats.avgAge: No strict age peak; affects children through adults in endemic agricultural communities
keyStats.globalCases: True global burden underestimated due to limited surveillance; concentrated in Middle East, Central Asia, and parts of East Africa

definition: Trichostrongyliasis (ICD-10: B81.2) is a parasitic intestinal infection caused by several species of the genus Trichostrongylus, small nematodes in the family Trichostrongylidae that primarily parasitise the small intestine of ruminants and other herbivores, with humans as occasional hosts. The most clinically relevant human-infecting species are T. orientalis, T. colubriformis, T. axei, T. vitrinus, and T. capricola. The parasite is classified among the soil-transmitted helminths, though its life cycle differs from that of Ascaris or hookworm: larvae are shed in animal faeces, develop to the infective third stage (L3) on moist soil, and contaminate grazing grass, raw leafy vegetables, and water sources. Humans acquire infection by swallowing L3 larvae on inadequately washed raw vegetables, contaminated water, or soil via hand-to-mouth contact in agricultural settings. After ingestion, larvae undergo limited skin-penetration phases in the intestinal mucosa before returning to the lumen to mature into adults. Adult worms attach to and burrow briefly into the duodenal and jejunal mucosa, causing mucosal erosion and a degree of blood loss that, in heavy infections (over several hundred worms), leads to microcytic or normocytic anaemia and hypoalbuminaemia. The infection falls under infectious diseases and is clinically important in resource-limited settings where animal husbandry is practiced without adequate sanitation. Diagnosis requires careful stool microscopy because Trichostrongylus eggs closely resemble those of hookworm, leading to frequent misidentification in routine laboratory practice.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation, occurring in people with light worm burdens (fewer than 50-100 adult worms); no clinical symptoms but eggs are detectable on stool microscopy.
Heavier infections producing epigastric discomfort, nausea, diarrhoea, flatulence, and weight loss; associated with peripheral eosinophilia and variable degrees of anaemia.

Severe infections, more common in children and malnourished individuals, in which the cumulative blood and protein loss from mucosal erosion leads to clinically significant iron-deficiency or normocytic anaemia, hypoalbuminaemia, and growth faltering.

primarySymptoms: Epigastric or periumbilical cramping pain, often worse in the morning before eating, caused by adult worms burrowing into and irritating the duodenal and jejunal mucosa.
Nausea and, in some patients, vomiting, occurring particularly in heavier infections when mucosal irritation is greatest, and tending to be worse in the morning.
Diarrhoea or loose stools, sometimes alternating with normal bowel habit; watery diarrhoea is more common in heavy infections and in children.
Fatigue and weakness, which are disproportionate to the degree of visible gastrointestinal symptoms in patients with anaemia from chronic intestinal blood loss.
Pallor, breathlessness on exertion, and dizziness in patients with significant anaemia — features that are more prominent in children and malnourished individuals with heavy worm burdens.
Anorexia and reduced appetite, contributing to the nutritional deficit in children in endemic areas where infection co-occurs with other soil-transmitted helminths.
Weight loss or failure to thrive in children with heavy, prolonged, untreated infections, reflecting both anorexia and the intestinal protein and blood losses.

Peripheral blood eosinophilia, detected on a full blood count; typically mild (5-15% of white cells) and reflects the host immune response to larval tissue migration during the early phase of infection.
Flatulence and abdominal bloating, common but nonspecific symptoms of intestinal mucosal irritation by the adult worms.
Pruritus ani (itching around the anus) in some patients, though less specific and prominent than in enterobiasis; probably related to mucosal irritation rather than perianal egg deposition.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent morning epigastric discomfort or nausea without a clear cause in someone with agricultural or rural livestock exposure.
Unexplained mild anaemia on routine bloods in a person from an endemic region or who has returned from one.
Progressive fatigue and pallor in a child from a rural agricultural community, particularly when other household members are also symptomatic.
Unexpected peripheral eosinophilia on a blood count done for another reason in a person with potential soil or animal exposure.
emergencySigns: Severe anaemia with haemoglobin below 7 g/dL causing chest pain, marked dyspnoea, or haemodynamic instability — particularly a risk in children with heavy, prolonged infection.
Profuse watery diarrhoea with signs of dehydration: sunken eyes, dry mouth, decreased urine output, or altered alertness in a young child.
Severe protein-energy malnutrition with oedema (kwashiorkor-like picture) in a child from an endemic area with heavy co-helminth infection.

causes.cause: Ingestion of infective third-stage larvae on contaminated raw vegetables
causes.description: The primary route in most cases: L3 larvae shed in the faeces of cattle, sheep, goats, and other ruminants develop on moist soil and contaminate the surface of leafy vegetables grown in or irrigated with faecally contaminated water. Inadequate washing of salad leaves, herbs, and other raw produce before consumption allows larvae to be swallowed.

causes.cause: Consumption of contaminated water from ponds, streams, or unprotected wells
causes.description: In communities where animal grazing occurs near water sources, L3 larvae may be present in surface water used for drinking or washing vegetables. Drinking unboiled or unfiltered water in such settings provides a secondary transmission route.

causes.cause: Hand-to-mouth transfer of larvae in agricultural workers
causes.description: Farm workers handling animal bedding, manure, or soil in endemic areas may transfer L3 larvae from contaminated hands to the mouth, particularly in the absence of handwashing facilities or protective gloves.

causes.cause: Close contact with infected ruminant animals
causes.description: Trichostrongylus species primarily parasitise grazing animals; communities that keep livestock in or very close to living spaces have higher environmental larval contamination and thus higher human infection rates than those with separated animal husbandry.

causes.cause: Inadequate sanitation allowing animal faecal contamination of food gardens
causes.description: In areas lacking proper toilet facilities and animal waste management, human and animal faeces may contaminate vegetable plots, establishing a persistent environmental reservoir of larvae that sustains ongoing transmission in the community.

riskFactors.factor: Agricultural or pastoral occupation involving livestock contact
riskFactors.description: Farmers and herders who work closely with sheep, goats, cattle, or horses in endemic regions have the highest infection rates; studies in Iran have found prevalence rates of 10-40% in such communities.

Eating salad greens, herbs, watercress, or other raw vegetables grown in or irrigated with faecally contaminated soil or water carries the highest food-source risk; thorough washing reduces but does not entirely eliminate larval contamination.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in rural endemic areas (Middle East, Central Asia, parts of Africa)
riskFactors.description: Endemicity is closely tied to livestock-keeping practices and sanitation infrastructure; rural communities in Iran, Iraq, Egypt, Ethiopia, and parts of East Asia show consistently higher prevalence than urban populations in the same countries.

riskFactors.factor: Young children with frequent soil contact
riskFactors.description: Young children in endemic areas have high rates of soil ingestion through play and hand-to-mouth habits, and their smaller blood volume means even light infections can produce clinically significant anaemia more quickly than in adults.

Iron and protein deficiency, common in the same communities where trichostrongyliasis is endemic, amplify the impact of intestinal blood loss and protein leak from worm-mediated mucosal erosion, producing more severe anaemia and malnutrition.

riskFactors.factor: Absence of safe water and sanitation
riskFactors.description: Lack of piped water, sealed latrines, and proper waste disposal for animal faeces creates persistent environmental larval contamination of food and water sources; sanitation improvement is the most effective long-term control measure.

Trichostrongyliasis frequently co-occurs with hookworm, ascariasis, and trichuriasis in the same endemic populations; combined helminth burdens significantly worsen anaemia, malnutrition, and developmental outcomes, especially in children.

affectedDemographics: Rural agricultural communities in Iran, Iraq, Egypt, Ethiopia, and parts of East and South-East Asia
Livestock farmers and herders with regular close animal contact
School-aged children in endemic areas with frequent soil exposure
Subsistence farmers consuming home-grown raw vegetables irrigated with untreated water
Travellers who have consumed raw vegetables or water in endemic regions
People living in communities with limited access to safe water and sanitation
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of trichostrongyliasis depends on stool microscopy, which is the cornerstone of confirmation. The eggs of Trichostrongylus species are elongated and pointed at both ends, measuring 73-95 × 40-50 micrometres, and closely resemble hookworm eggs (Necator americanus, Ancylostoma duodenale) — the key difference is size (Trichostrongylus eggs are larger and more tapered) and the advanced segmentation of the embryo at the time of passage. Misidentification as hookworm is common in routine laboratories, and the clinical implications differ because hookworm treatment with albendazole covers both genera, but awareness affects epidemiological tracking. A formalin-ethyl acetate stool concentration technique or Kato-Katz preparation increases sensitivity for light infections. Standard single stool examination detects approximately 50-70% of infections; examination of three consecutive samples improves this to over 90%. Stool culture to hatch larvae (Harada-Mori or agar plate culture) allows species-level identification based on larval morphology and is available in specialist parasitology laboratories. Peripheral blood eosinophilia is present in the majority of symptomatic patients and supports the diagnosis but is neither sensitive nor specific. A full blood count demonstrating microcytic or normocytic anaemia, combined with low serum ferritin or albumin, suggests heavy infection with nutritional impact. Molecular identification (PCR) using genus-specific primers can precisely identify Trichostrongylus species and distinguish them from hookworm, but is currently confined to research and specialist reference settings. The differential diagnosis includes hookworm infection, Strongyloides stercoralis, Ascaris lumbricoides, giardiasis, peptic ulcer disease, and, in children, other causes of nutritional anaemia.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool microscopy (direct smear and formalin-ethyl acetate concentration)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The primary diagnostic method; demonstrates elongated, pointed Trichostrongylus eggs, which are larger than hookworm eggs and show advanced embryonic segmentation at time of stool passage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You provide a small fresh stool sample in a sealed container. The laboratory processes and examines it under a microscope. Examining three samples on different days improves detection from about 60% to over 90%.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A quantitative smear technique that counts eggs per gram of stool, enabling assessment of infection intensity; important for research and identifying patients needing treatment in control programmes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard stool sample is processed at the laboratory; results available the same day in laboratories equipped for this technique.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool larval culture (Harada-Mori or agar plate culture)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Allows the embryonated eggs to hatch into larvae whose morphology identifies the specific Trichostrongylus species and distinguishes them definitively from hookworm larvae.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The stool sample is incubated at room temperature for 7-10 days; the laboratory then examines the resulting larvae microscopically. Not available in all centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects peripheral eosinophilia (supporting parasitic infection) and anaemia (indicating significant worm burden with blood or protein loss).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw from the arm; results usually available within hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum ferritin, iron studies, and albumin
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses the nutritional impact of infection; low ferritin and albumin suggest heavy intestinal blood and protein loss requiring treatment and nutritional support.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood samples sent to laboratory; results available the same day.

treatmentOverview: Trichostrongyliasis is highly treatable with a short course of oral anthelmintic drugs. Albendazole 400 mg as a single dose or mebendazole 100-500 mg is effective against Trichostrongylus species, achieving cure rates of approximately 70-90% per single treatment. Pyrantel pamoate at 11 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) is an alternative that is effective and widely available over the counter in some countries. Unlike Ascaris and Trichuris, which show high single-dose efficacy with albendazole, Trichostrongylus may sometimes require a repeat dose or a longer course in heavy infections. The response to treatment should be assessed by repeat stool examination 3-4 weeks after treatment; persistent egg positivity warrants a second course. In patients with iron-deficiency anaemia from intestinal blood loss, anthelmintic treatment alone may not fully correct the anaemia, and supplemental iron (ferrous sulphate 200 mg three times daily for 3 months, or equivalent) should be co-prescribed. Malnourished children may also require nutritional rehabilitation alongside parasite treatment. In endemic communities, mass drug administration (MDA) programmes using albendazole or mebendazole as part of soil-transmitted helminth control can reduce community prevalence, though coverage of Trichostrongylus is sometimes incomplete because it is less well profiled in standard MDA targeting than Ascaris or hookworm. Improving sanitation, access to safe water, and washing of raw vegetables are the long-term community-level interventions that reduce reinfection after treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: The preferred first-line treatment: a single 400 mg oral dose in adults and children over 2 years. Albendazole inhibits tubulin polymerisation, impairing worm glucose absorption and motility. Well tolerated; mild nausea or abdominal discomfort in fewer than 5% of recipients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose cure rates approximately 70-90% against Trichostrongylus; a second dose at 2 weeks is recommended if repeat stool microscopy at 3-4 weeks remains positive.

medicalTreatments.name: Mebendazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative first-line option: 100-500 mg as a single dose, or 100 mg twice daily for 3 days in heavy infections. Like albendazole, it acts via tubulin inhibition. Minimal systemic absorption makes it very safe.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single-dose efficacy approximately 60-80% for Trichostrongylus; slightly lower than for Ascaris or Trichuris with the same regimen.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyrantel pamoate
medicalTreatments.description: 11 mg/kg (maximum 1 g) as a single oral dose; available over the counter in several countries. Acts by causing spastic paralysis of the worm. An alternative when albendazole and mebendazole are unavailable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 75-90% for trichostrongyliasis; broadly similar to mebendazole.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron supplementation
medicalTreatments.description: Oral ferrous sulphate (or equivalent) co-prescribed with anthelmintic therapy in patients with documented iron-deficiency anaemia. Duration typically 3 months; dosage adjusted for age and weight. Corrects the haematological deficit that anthelmintic treatment alone does not fully reverse.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Iron supplementation increases haemoglobin by approximately 1-2 g/dL per month in deficient patients; combination with anthelmintic therapy is more effective than either alone at improving anaemia outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Thorough vegetable washing and peeling
alternativeTreatments.description: Washing raw vegetables under running water and, where possible, peeling the outer leaves substantially reduces the number of larvae present; not a treatment for established infection but an effective preventive measure for reinfection.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary protein and nutritional support
alternativeTreatments.description: For children with heavy infections and growth faltering, high-protein nutritional support (pulses, eggs, dairy) alongside anthelmintic therapy accelerates recovery of lean body mass and supports immune function.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cases of trichostrongyliasis in adults with light infections can be managed in primary care with a course of albendazole and dietary counselling. Infectious disease specialist input is appropriate when anaemia is clinically significant, when infection persists after two adequate anthelmintic courses, when the laboratory diagnosis is uncertain (particularly in distinguishing Trichostrongylus from hookworm), or when the patient is immunocompromised. Paediatric specialist involvement is warranted when a child presents with growth faltering, severe anaemia (haemoglobin below 8 g/dL), or co-morbid malnutrition.
doctorSelectionGuide: A general practitioner or family physician can diagnose and treat uncomplicated trichostrongyliasis. For patients with persistent infection, heavy anaemia, or uncertain diagnosis, referral to an infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist with access to a specialist parasitology laboratory is recommended. In endemic settings, community health workers and local public health programmes deliver most treatment through mass drug administration.
preventionStrategies: Wash all raw vegetables, herbs, and salad leaves thoroughly under running water before eating; peeling outer leaves of leafy vegetables reduces larval load.
Drink only boiled, filtered, or treated water in areas where open-water sources may be contaminated by livestock.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water before preparing or eating food, and after handling soil, animals, or animal waste.
Keep animals away from vegetable gardens and ensure animal faeces are disposed of away from food-growing areas.
Participate in community deworming programmes where they exist; periodic albendazole or mebendazole administration substantially reduces community worm burdens.
Improve sanitation by using latrines and managing animal waste to reduce environmental contamination of soil and water.
lifestyleModifications: In endemic areas, peel or thoroughly wash all raw vegetables and avoid watercress and herbs from streams or open-water areas.
Use gloves when working in soil or handling animal manure; wash hands promptly afterwards.
Ensure children wash their hands before eating and after play in soil, particularly in farming households.
Complete the full anthelmintic course and attend for repeat stool testing 3-4 weeks after treatment to confirm clearance.
Maintain an iron-rich diet (meat, legumes, leafy greens, fortified cereals) while taking iron supplements during the correction of anaemia.
Consider household and community prevention — if one household member is diagnosed, others in the same living and agricultural environment should be screened.

recommended: Iron-rich foods such as red meat, liver, legumes, leafy green vegetables, and iron-fortified cereals to support anaemia recovery
High-protein foods to replace protein lost through intestinal mucosal damage: eggs, dairy, pulses, and fish
Cooked vegetables where possible during active infection, reverting to well-washed raw vegetables after clearance
Adequate fluid intake to support recovery and prevent dehydration during diarrhoeal episodes
avoid: Raw, unwashed vegetables from potentially contaminated soil or irrigated with untreated water
Watercress and aquatic herbs from streams or rivers in pastoral areas
Untreated surface water from rivers, ponds, or open wells in endemic areas

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity does not need to be restricted in trichostrongyliasis. During active symptomatic infection with anaemia or diarrhoea, light activity is appropriate and strenuous exertion should be deferred until symptoms and haemoglobin improve. Once the anthelmintic course is complete and anaemia is resolving, normal activity should be encouraged; gradual return to heavy physical work is appropriate 4-8 weeks after starting iron supplementation, when haemoglobin levels are measurably improving.
dailyManagement: Take the full prescribed anthelmintic course; for heavy infections a second dose 2 weeks after the first may be recommended by the clinician.
Take iron supplements consistently — daily, with food to reduce gastrointestinal side effects — for the full prescribed duration of typically 3 months.
Attend for repeat stool testing 3-4 weeks after completing anthelmintic treatment to confirm egg clearance.
Monitor haemoglobin at baseline, 6 weeks, and 3 months to assess anaemia recovery during iron supplementation.
Apply dietary and environmental measures to prevent reinfection, since anthelmintic treatment does not confer immunity.
prognosis: The prognosis for trichostrongyliasis is excellent in individuals who receive appropriate anthelmintic treatment. Cure rates exceed 80% after a correctly administered single-dose course; a second dose achieves clearance in the majority of persistent cases. Anaemia resolves within 3-4 months with combined anthelmintic and iron therapy. In children with heavy, long-standing untreated infections, there may be modest deficits in cognitive development and physical growth that partially reverse with treatment and nutritional rehabilitation, though full catch-up depends on the severity and duration of the nutritional impact. Reinfection is common in the absence of improved sanitation and food hygiene, particularly in endemic rural communities, and does not indicate treatment failure. No chronic organ damage attributable specifically to Trichostrongylus has been demonstrated in humans at typical infection intensities.
recoveryTimeline: Gastrointestinal symptoms — abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea — typically begin to resolve within 3-7 days of starting anthelmintic treatment as worms die and mucosal inflammation settles. Peripheral eosinophilia normalises within 4-8 weeks. Haemoglobin begins to rise measurably within 4-6 weeks of starting combined anthelmintic and iron therapy and reaches normal levels in most patients within 3 months. Repeat stool microscopy at 3-4 weeks confirms egg clearance; if eggs persist, a second treatment course is given.
complications: Iron-deficiency or normocytic anaemia from chronic mucosal blood loss, clinically significant in heavy infections and in children and women with pre-existing iron deficiency.
Hypoalbuminaemia and protein-energy malnutrition in severe or prolonged infections, contributing to oedema, immune suppression, and impaired wound healing.
Growth faltering and cognitive impairment in children with heavy, untreated infections, particularly when co-infected with other soil-transmitted helminths.
Persistent or recurrent infection from reinfection in endemic communities without improved sanitation, requiring repeated treatment courses.
Misdiagnosis as hookworm infection due to egg morphological similarity, which is clinically minor since treatment overlaps, but has public health surveillance implications.

supportResources.name: CDC — Trichostrongyliasis
supportResources.description: Overview of trichostrongyliasis epidemiology, biology, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

supportResources.name: WHO — Soil-transmitted helminthiases
supportResources.description: WHO fact sheet on soil-transmitted helminths including treatment, preventive chemotherapy, and global control targets.

supportResources.name: WHO Model Formulary — Anthelmintics
supportResources.description: WHO guidance on anthelmintic drug dosing including albendazole, mebendazole, and pyrantel for intestinal nematode infections.

sources.title: Bethony J et al. Soil-transmitted helminth infections: ascariasis, trichuriasis, and hookworm. Lancet, 2006
Khuroo MS. Trichostrongyliasis: diagnosis and treatment. World Journal of Gastroenterology, 2014
WHO — Preventive chemotherapy in human helminthiasis: coordinated use of anthelmintic drugs

Ananthakrishnan S, Gafur A. Intestinal nematode infections in South Asia: an updated review. Tropical Parasitology, 2021

confusedWithConditions.name: Amebiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amebiasis is caused by a protozoan, not a nematode, and presents with dysentery and bloody diarrhoea; diagnosis is by stool antigen test or microscopy for Entamoeba cysts, not elongated nematode eggs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer causes epigastric pain relieved by antacids, not associated with eosinophilia or positive stool microscopy for worm eggs; it is diagnosed by endoscopy showing mucosal ulceration.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease is chronic, relapsing, and autoimmune; it does not produce worm eggs on stool microscopy and is not linked to agricultural exposure or livestock contact.

confusedWithConditions.name: Enterobiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pinworm infection causes perianal pruritus and disrupted sleep rather than anaemia and epigastric pain; it is diagnosed by the tape test, not stool egg microscopy.

relatedConditions.name: Anisakiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another intestinal nematode infection requiring anthelmintic treatment; both are managed within the infectious disease specialty and share the anthelmintic drug class.

relatedConditions.name: Gnathostomiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A more invasive intestinal nematode acquired from animal exposure; both are nematode infections in the same ICD-10 category.

relatedConditions.name: Visceral larva migrans
relatedConditions.relevance: Another nematode condition producing eosinophilia from larval tissue migration; co-endemic in agricultural regions where animal contact is frequent.

relatedConditions.name: Trichuriasis (whipworm infection)
relatedConditions.relevance: A frequently co-occurring soil-transmitted helminth infection in the same endemic communities; both cause intestinal symptoms and anaemia, and both respond to albendazole or mebendazole.

faqs.question: What is trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: Trichostrongyliasis is an intestinal worm infection caused by Trichostrongylus species — small nematodes primarily found in livestock. Humans become infected by accidentally swallowing larvae on raw unwashed vegetables or from contaminated water in farming communities. It causes abdominal pain, diarrhoea, and sometimes anaemia.

faqs.question: How do you get trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: Infection occurs by swallowing infective larvae on raw vegetables grown in soil contaminated by animal faeces, or from drinking untreated water near livestock. It is not spread from person to person. Agricultural workers and rural communities in the Middle East, Central Asia, and parts of Africa are most commonly affected.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: Many people with light infections have no symptoms. Heavier infections cause epigastric or abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, fatigue, and weight loss. Significant worm loads can cause mild anaemia from intestinal blood loss, particularly in children and malnourished individuals.

faqs.question: How is trichostrongyliasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is by stool microscopy, which identifies the distinctive elongated Trichostrongylus eggs. Three consecutive stool samples improve detection to over 90%. The eggs look similar to hookworm eggs and are sometimes misidentified; a specialist parasitology laboratory is needed for definitive species identification.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: A single oral dose of albendazole (400 mg) or mebendazole (100-500 mg) is the standard treatment, curing approximately 70-90% of cases. A repeat dose at 2-4 weeks may be needed for heavy infections. Iron supplements are co-prescribed if anaemia is present.

faqs.question: Is trichostrongyliasis the same as hookworm?
faqs.answer: No. They are different nematode genera. Trichostrongylus and hookworm (Necator and Ancylostoma) eggs look similar on stool microscopy, which causes frequent misidentification. Both cause intestinal symptoms and anaemia, and both are treated with albendazole or mebendazole, so the clinical management overlaps even if the species differ.

faqs.question: Can trichostrongyliasis cause anaemia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Heavy worm burdens erode the intestinal mucosa, causing ongoing blood and protein loss. This leads to iron-deficiency or normocytic anaemia, most severe in children and malnourished individuals. Iron supplementation alongside anthelmintic treatment is needed to fully correct the anaemia.

faqs.question: Is trichostrongyliasis dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most healthy adults with light infections, trichostrongyliasis causes minor symptoms or none at all and is easily cured. In children with heavy infections it can cause significant anaemia and growth faltering, making treatment important. Serious complications are uncommon with adequate treatment.

faqs.question: How long does treatment take to work?
faqs.answer: Gastrointestinal symptoms typically begin improving within 3-7 days of a single anthelmintic dose as worms die. Anaemia takes longer to correct — haemoglobin begins rising measurably within 4-6 weeks when iron supplements are co-prescribed and usually normalises within 3 months.

faqs.question: Can trichostrongyliasis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anthelmintic treatment clears the current infection but does not prevent reinfection from a contaminated environment. In endemic communities, reinfection from unwashed vegetables, contaminated water, or soil exposure is common. Environmental and hygiene measures are essential to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Where is trichostrongyliasis most common?
faqs.answer: Trichostrongyliasis is most prevalent in rural agricultural communities in Iran, Iraq, and Egypt in the Middle East, and in parts of Central Asia, East Africa, and South-East Asia. It occurs worldwide but is uncommon in settings without close human-livestock contact and without raw vegetable consumption from contaminated soil.

faqs.question: Does eosinophilia always mean a worm infection?
faqs.answer: Elevated eosinophils on a blood count are common with many parasitic infections, including trichostrongyliasis, but also occur in allergic conditions, asthma, and other helminth infections. Stool microscopy and a detailed exposure history are needed to identify the specific cause in someone with unexplained eosinophilia.

faqs.question: Do I need to treat the whole household?
faqs.answer: Unlike pinworm, trichostrongyliasis is not transmitted person-to-person; household members are not automatically at risk from a single case. However, if multiple people in the same agricultural household share a common exposure source (the same vegetable garden or water supply), simultaneous testing and treatment of all symptomatic members is sensible.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine against trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: No vaccine is available for human trichostrongyliasis. Prevention relies on safe food handling — washing raw vegetables thoroughly — drinking treated water, handwashing, and, in endemic communities, periodic anthelmintic treatment as part of deworming programmes.

faqs.question: What vegetables are most risky for trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: Leafy vegetables grown in soil fertilised with animal manure, watercress harvested from streams near grazing land, and fresh herbs from irrigated plots in endemic areas carry the highest risk. Thorough washing under running water and, where possible, brief blanching substantially reduces larval contamination.

faqs.question: How does trichostrongyliasis affect children?
faqs.answer: Children are more vulnerable to the consequences of trichostrongyliasis than adults because their smaller blood volume means even moderate intestinal blood loss produces clinically significant anaemia more quickly. Heavy chronic infections in childhood can impair school performance, physical growth, and immune function, particularly when combined with other worm infections or malnutrition.

faqs.question: What blood test findings suggest trichostrongyliasis?
faqs.answer: Peripheral eosinophilia — typically 5-15% of white blood cells — is the most common finding, reflecting the immune response to larval tissue migration. Iron-deficiency or normocytic anaemia and low serum albumin indicate significant worm burden. These findings support the diagnosis but are not specific; stool microscopy confirms it.

faqs.question: Can trichostrongyliasis be caught from eating meat?
faqs.answer: No. Trichostrongyliasis is acquired from larvae on contaminated vegetables, water, or soil — not from eating meat, even from infected animals. The larvae do not encyst in meat as some other parasites do; they live in the gut of the animal host.

metaDescription: Trichostrongyliasis is an intestinal worm infection causing abdominal pain and anaemia. Learn stool microscopy diagnosis, albendazole treatment, and prevention.
